items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.q: What does in-order traversal of a binary search tree produce?
items.misconception: Nodes in the order they were inserted.
items.why: Students forget the BST property: left subtree less than node less than right subtree. In-order (left, node, right) therefore visits values in sorted order - regardless of insertion order.

items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.
Correct answer: Stack. “Queue” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the time complexity of indexed access in an array?
items.why: It's O(1). “O(log n)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a stack has 5 items and you push 3 then pop 2, how many items remain?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
